calendar.matters:
MOTION TO QUASH SUBPOENAS
Off Calendar Apr-28-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances (by Zoom):
Mark Boskovich, Esq. 408-289-1417 Cerri Boskovich & Allard for Plaintiff.
Christopher Egan, Esq. 916-929-1481, Porter Scott for Defendant Jason Davis
Marisa Sanchez, Esq. 909-243-3286 Taylor Anderson for Defendant San Francisco Waldorf School Association (in-person)

ARGUED.

Mr. Egan contests the tentative ruling and presents arguments; discovery inadmissible, relevance of subpoena not standard, issues on privacy rights, pending criminal case from 20 years ago unduly prejudicial, time consuming, fishing.
Court comments and poses questions.
Mr. Boskovich presents arguments; clients to be witnesses in Mendocino, coordination of investigation and connection of cases, there may be evidence which are admissible, grooming tactics relevant.

The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

Defendant Jason Davis's motion to quash deposition subpoenas for production of business records to San Francisco Police Department, Mendocino County Sheriff's Office, and Ukiah Police Department.

Taken Under Submission.*** The Court will prepare an order.

Judge: Richard C. Darwin, Clerk: M. Goodman, Reported. (302/RCD)

***After the hearing, the court signs an order. (see scanned order for details)***

Notice Of Motion To Stay Civil Proceedings
Off Calendar Apr-22-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant Davis's renewed motion for stay of all proceedings is DENIED.

The court concludes that the criminal complaint filed on November 19, 2025, is a new circumstance to warrant renewal of this motion. (CCP 1008(b).)

In Avant! Corp v. Sup. Crt, (2000) 79 Cal.App.4th 876, 885, the court listed the following factors when ruling on the instant motion: (1) defendant's Fifth Amendment interest; (2) the interest of plaintiff in proceeding expeditiously and conducting discovery and the potential prejudice to plaintiffs of a delay; (3) the convenience of the court in the management of its cases, and the efficient use of judicial resources; (4) the interests of persons not parties to the civil litigation; and (5) the interest of the public in the pending civil and criminal litigation.

Here, these factors weigh in favor of denying this motion. A blanket stay of the entire action as to all defendants (including the school/tutoring defendants) would unduly prejudice plaintiffs. Defendant Davis can invoke his Fifth Amendment privilege as to particular discovery questions. (Fuller v. Superior Court (2001) 87 Cal.App.4th 299, 307-308.) "The trial court must be given the opportunity to determine whether particular questions posed in the depositions would elicit answers that support a conviction or that furnish a link in the chain of evidence needed to prosecute the witness, and which may thus be subject to constitutional protection." (Id. at 308.) As this court has already noted, plaintiffs have already waited two decades and there is an interest in moving this case forward.

A proposed order is submitted for the court's review.

Judge: Ronald E. Quidachay, Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/REQ)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Nov-13-2025 Set For Trial On Dec-14-26 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Oct-02-2025 Continued To Nov-26-25 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Stay Civil Proceedings. Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof, And Declaration Of Eric Schattl
Off Calendar Sep-12-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant Jason Davis's motion to stay civil proceedings is DENIED. Defendant Davis failed to provide any evidence in support of his motion.

Davis requests that these proceedings be stayed out of a concern that testimony he could be compelled to give in this case could be used against him in a potential criminal case -- which has yet to be brought against him.

Davis has the burden to demonstrate a reasonable belief that his testimony in the civil case will result in him incriminating himself. (Blackburn v. Superior Court (1993) 21 Cal.App.4th 414, 431.) The court considers the factors laid out in Keating v. Office of Thrift Supervision to analyze the necessity for a stay, looking at: " '(1) the interest of the plaintiffs in proceeding expeditiously with this litigation or any particular aspect of it, and the potential prejudice to plaintiffs of a delay; (2) the burden which any particular aspect of the proceedings may impose on defendants; (3) the convenience of the court in the management of its cases, and the efficient use of judicial resources; (4) the interests of persons not parties to the civil litigation; and (5) the interest of the public in the pending civil and criminal litigation. [Citation.]' " (Avant! Corp. v. Superior Court (2000) 79 Cal.App.4th 876, 885, quoting Keating v. Office of Thrift Supervision (9th Cir. 1995) 45 F.3d 322, 324.)

Here, Plaintiffs have already been waiting over two decades since the original alleged incident, so there is a high interest in proceeding expeditiously with this litigation. There is not a great enough demonstration that the burden on Defendant would be particularly high. Additionally, while Davis claims that there is a high public interest in this case, he has failed to provide any evidence supporting this claim.

Ultimately, Davis does not cogently explain why the court should grant the stay. He fails to provide the court with any citations to the criminal statute of limitations for the prosecution he is concerned about, and he mentions a currently active police investigation, but provides no evidence, or even a declaration, demonstrating that the police have requested a statement and/or interviewed other individuals associated with him.

So far in this case, there have been no requests by the plaintiffs actually asking Davis to incriminate himself. The court is not required to stay civil proceedings when there is a pending criminal action unless the parties would be substantially prejudiced absent a stay. (Avant! Corp. v. Superior Court (2000) 79 Cal.App.4th 876, 885.) Here, there is currently no parallel criminal case, and the plaintiffs' interests in proceeding with their lawsuit override staying the proceedings because there is the possibility that a criminal case may be brought sometime in the future.

California Evidence Code Section 913 explicitly prohibits the trier of fact from drawing inferences from a witness asserting their Fifth Amendment right to refuse to disclose privileged or self-incriminating information. This code applies to both criminal and civil litigation. (People v. Holloway (2004) 33 Cal.4th 96, 131.) The court is not persuaded by Davis's argument to the contrary.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the court's ruling and must drop a hard courtesy copy in Dept. 302's inbox.

Judge: John M. True; Clerk: M. Macadangdang; Not Reported. (302/JMT)
